segments.text: Here is the opening move. Let's start by opening eBay Advanced Search. This gives the whole demo a place to stand.
segments.fallback_text: We start by opening eBay Advanced Search. That gives the workflow a stage.
segments.subtitle: Step 1: Open eBay Advanced Search

segments.text: With the setup in place, we can chase the signal. We enter main search keywords. This is the sort of repetition the workflow should steal from us.
segments.fallback_text: Next, we enter main search keywords. This is where repetition gets retired.
segments.subtitle: Step 2: Enter main search keywords

segments.text: Now the page has something to say. We change the keyword match dropdown to exact order.
segments.fallback_text: Change the keyword match dropdown to exact order.
segments.subtitle: Step 3: Change the keyword match dropdown to exact order

segments.text: This is where the pattern starts to show. We enter words to exclude from results.
segments.fallback_text: Next, we enter words to exclude from results. This is where repetition gets retired.

segments.text: Here the workflow stops posing and starts proving. We include title and description in the search scope.
segments.fallback_text: Include title and description in the search scope.
segments.subtitle: Step 5: Include title and description in the search scope

segments.text: Now the payoff is close enough to see. We set minimum and maximum price filters.
segments.fallback_text: Next, we set minimum and maximum price filters. That keeps the run honest.
segments.subtitle: Step 6: Set minimum and maximum price filters

segments.text: This is where the result starts to feel earned. We filter for new-condition items. It keeps the flow easy to follow without sounding like a checklist.

segments.text: That lands the result. We run the search and open the filtered results page. This is where all the quiet setup finally earns applause.
